content:
Forward-only migrations are fine until you need minute forty-one.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The deploy finished at 10:06 A.M. Metrics looked flat. Migrations ran green in CI, green in staging, green in production. We ate lunch feeling competent.
At 10:47 A.M. a race condition we had never seen in staging surfaced on a hot partition. Finance asked for rollback. Not a feature flag rollback. A real rollback. Put the old code back and put the database back where it was.
The old code came back in eleven minutes. The database did not.
Forward-only migration step three had dropped a column we still needed for the rollback binary sitting in the previous container image.
The migration succeeded. The rollback plan assumed archaeology that no longer existed.
Forward-Only Felt Modern Until It Didn’t
We chose expand-contract because zero-downtime guides told us to. Add new column. Dual-write. Backfill. Switch reads. Switch writes. Drop old column. Clean.
Steps one through four worked. Step five removed the escape hatch while the new code still had latent bugs.
Staging had never kept the old column long enough to matter. Production traffic had shapes staging pretended did not exist. The rollback playbook still said “redeploy previous image” like the schema was a time machine.
Warm truth: forward-only is not the same as forward-only-with-no-exit. You can move forward responsibly and still leave a door until the new path survives a bad day.
Minute Forty-One Is When Plans Get Honest
Minute zero through forty are demos and dashboards. Minute forty-one is when rare overlaps meet real money.
Our bug needed a column that stored legacy invoice grouping. New code computed grouping differently and mostly ignored the old field. Mostly was the problem.
Rollback engineers tried:
→ Restore column from backup — backup retention shorter than we remembered for that table → Re-add column via migration — data gone, downstream jobs already rewrote history → Feature flag — flag assumed both schemas, not missing columns
We spent the afternoon reconstructing state from audit logs while support handled angry finance users. The fix forward was correct. The rollback fantasy was expensive theater.
What a Credible Rollback Narrative Requires
Afterward we wrote rollback criteria the way we write SLOs: specific and testable.
Before dropping anything, ask:
→ Can previous production image start against current schema without manual surgery? → Do we have a reversible window measured in days, not minutes? → Did we run rollback drill in staging with production-scale row counts? → Are async consumers compatible with both shapes during the window? → Who signs off on irreversible steps separately from “migration succeeded”?
If any answer is “we hope,” you do not have a rollback plan. You have optimism.
Expand-Contract With an Honest Kill Switch
Patterns that helped the next migration:
→ Keep deprecated columns until two successful deploy cycles plus one scheduled game day → Tag migrations reversible-until with calendar date in the migration file name → Block irreversible migrations on Fridays unless on-call opts in writing → Pair schema change with feature flag that can route around new logic without old column dependency → Document "rollback stops here" in the ADR, not only in the runbook nobody opened
We also stopped letting agents draft irreversible SQL without human review of the delete steps. Generated migrations love to drop things confidently.
The Human Cost of “Already Migrated”
Teams celebrate forward progress. Fair. Celebrating before the new path survives rollback pressure trains org muscle memory to treat schema deletion as completion.
Engineers on that incident remembered the day as “we told finance rollback was impossible because we deleted their column.” Trust erodes faster than schemas change.
Kindness to future on-call means leaving boring artifacts: columns, flags, compatibility views, comments that say when removal is allowed.
Teaching Rollback Without Fear Mongering.
This is not an argument against shipping. Ship. Just do not confuse “migration applied” with “migration safe to keep.”
Run one rollback drill per quarter on a non-critical service until muscle memory exists. Time how long it takes. Put the number in the ADR. Numbers convince planners more than principles.
Forward-only migrations are fine until you need minute forty-one. Prepare for minute forty-one and the forward path gets faster, not slower, because people trust the gates.
What We Do Before Any Irreversible Step Now
Every migration ticket with a drop or rename includes a rollback drill checklist signed by on-call, not only by the author.
The drill is blunt: deploy previous image against current schema in staging, run synthetic transactions, capture errors. If errors appear, irreversible step waits. No hero merges on Friday.
We also keep compatibility views longer than feels elegant. Views are cheap. Reconstructing dropped columns from audit logs is not cheap.
Finance asked why migrations slowed slightly. We showed the rollback drill minutes versus the half-day rebuild. They stopped asking.
Kindness to future on-call is a line item, not a vibe.
The Expand Phase Nobody Celebrates.
Expand phases feel like progress because schema grows. Contract phases feel like progress because traffic moves. The middle dual-write phase feels like friction because everything is duplicated and boring.
That boring middle is where rollback still lives. We now celebrate middle phases explicitly in status updates: “still reversible until Friday” is a success state, not stagnation.
Engineers rushing to drop columns are often chasing closure feelings, not risk reduction. Managers can reward reversible milestones the same way they reward irreversible ones.
Warm reminder: the customer does not care that your migration graph looked elegant in the doc. They care that invoices stay correct when you revert a bad deploy at minute forty-one.
Staging Lied Because Volume Lied.
Our staging database had ten thousand rows in the hot partition table. Production had nine million. The race we hit at minute forty-one required concurrent backfill workers and live writes colliding on the same index range. Staging never exercised that collision because staging never tried.
After the incident we added a rule: any migration touching a hot partition runs once against a restored production snapshot subset in a sandbox, even if CI already greenlit logic tests. Extra hours upfront. Fewer finance pages later.
We also document which rollback steps require data repair versus image rollback. Those are different buttons. Mixing them under pressure wastes the eleven minutes you earned by keeping the old container image around.
The warm part: nobody on the team was careless. We followed the guide we had. The guide assumed rollback meant schema time travel. Production had moved forward-only because forward-only felt modern. Modern without an exit is just faster regret.
Teach juniors that migration success is a phase, not a ceremony. The drop statement is not confetti. It is a door closing.
I keep a printed sticky on my monitor now: reversible until proven otherwise. It sounds sentimental. It saved us from another column drop last month when someone tried to rush step five for a conference deadline.
Rollback drills feel embarrassing until the day finance asks for minute forty-one and you can say yes with a straight face. Schedule the embarrassment on purpose.
Write the rollback story before you write the forward migration. If the rollback story sounds like fantasy, the forward migration is not ready, no matter what CI says.
Warm teams still ship fast. They just stop calling irreversible steps “done” when only the forward path has been exercised.
Bring finance into the reversible window conversation once. They understand option value better than we give them credit for.
Minute forty-one is not superstition. It is the moment the demo ends and production keeps running.
Keep the old schema until the new code survives a bad day, not until the sprint demo looks good.
Reversible is a feature. Treat it like one in planning, not like guilt. Your future on-call will thank you.
Forward-only migrations are fine until you need minute forty-one.
For migration patterns, failure recovery, and backend design that survives bad afternoons, The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is the reference I wish we had open before step five dropped that column.
Practice the “what breaks at rollback” muscle on Froquiz — 10,000+ questions across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Java, Python, JavaScript, React, microservices, plus Senior Dev Challenge scenarios built like migration war games, not textbook recitals.
That discipline costs calendar time upfront and returns sleep during the rollback you hope never comes.
→ Froquiz
